Critical — act now
HOME-ARP comment window is live — closes May 26, 2026. COS is collecting public comment on the HOME-ARP Allocation Plan amendment via an ArcGIS Survey123 form Esri's own ACR rates as "Partially Supports" on WCAG 2.1 A/AA. A WCAG 2.1 AA Drupal Webform replacement is ready to install — bilingual EN/ES, JSON webhook + staff email + native storage, inline accommodation intake.

249 of 284 metro districts have no website. CORA request is the only way a resident can access their own district's records.

See district audit →
WS Districts (WSDM) serves 30+ districts on a single WordPress site. Last calendar entry: 2021. No governance docs. No financials. One compliance letter fixes all 30+ simultaneously.

High priority
All posted PDFs likely fail WCAG 1.3.1. Meeting minutes, budgets, and audit docs are almost certainly untagged. This is the real HB21-1110 failure — not the homepage.

0 of 8 audited sites post their service plan. C.R.S. §32-1-202 requires it. This is the foundational legal document. Not one district links it publicly.

Resolves all seven critical/serious findings from the ADA audit: skip link, live regions, focus management, EN/ES localization, real-time validation messaging. LexisNexis ADAM Program dashboard — accessible rebuild is live. Audit and reference rebuild of the missing-children dashboard: APG-pattern tabs with roving tabindex and full keyboard nav, solid amber focus rings (3:1 against any surface), darker success-green for AA contrast, and "View Poster" placeholders converted from dead anchors to real buttons.

Shared · Developer compliance · April 2026

Wolfersberger Compliance & Bella Mesa Analysis

Metro district regulatory oversight gaps, Lorson Ranch board composition, and how Bella Mesa connects to the same industry-wide template.

Wolfersberger LLC — "Colorado Metro District Regulatory Oversight: Fact or Fiction?"
Source: wolfersbergerllc.com/metro-district-regulatory-oversight — a Colorado accounting and advocacy firm specializing in metro district reform. Wolfersberger has successfully assisted homeowners in recalling developer-controlled boards. Their findings are confirmed by CBS Colorado investigative reporting (March and May 2025) and the Bond Buyer (December 2024).
Six regulatory categories — every answer is "Nobody"
Who regulates metro district elections?
Nobody. No governmental agency oversees special district elections. All other Colorado government entities — cities, counties, state — are overseen by the Colorado Secretary of State. Special districts are explicitly carved out. Worse: violations of certain election laws in special district elections are EXEMPT from criminal prosecution per C.R.S. § 1-13.5-1601 and C.R.S. § 32-1-807. A resident's only recourse is to personally hire and fund a private attorney to sue.
Who regulates ethical conduct of board directors?
Nobody. Colorado's Independent Ethics Commission (IEC) — created by voter-approved Amendment 41 in 2006 — explicitly excludes metro districts from its jurisdiction. HB 25-1079 would have extended IEC oversight to special districts. It failed for the third consecutive year in May 2025. The Special District Association of Colorado opposed it. All other Colorado government officials — school boards, city councils, state executives — are covered. Metro district boards are not.
Who verifies directors are legally qualified to serve?
Nobody. When districts issue bonds, bond counsel attorneys state in their legal opinions that they simply rely on the directors' own representations that they qualify. Bond documents for Lorson Ranch — issued March 3, 2026 — followed this same pattern. No independent verification exists.
Who regulates compliance with CORA?
Nobody. No agency enforces CORA compliance for special districts. A resident who is denied records — as Emma Moore has been — must personally hire and fund a private attorney. The district has far more resources. This is structurally identical to LRMD1's $41.37/hour research fee and the Sheriff's disputed CORA response.
Who regulates public meeting requirements?
Nobody. Despite laws requiring meetings be held in accessible locations with proper notice, no agency enforces this. LRMD1's 2026 Transparency Notice lists meeting location as "Virtual" with no physical address and no scheduled regular meetings — only special meetings "as needed." This is the minimum legally permissible notice.
Who regulates service plan compliance?
Nobody. Cities and counties have "a long history of failing to monitor whether metro districts comply with their respective service plans," per Wolfersberger. When a developer-controlled board violates its service plan, the violations typically benefit the directors directly — and no agency intervenes.
The structural problem Wolfersberger identifies
Colorado Title 32 statutes give land developers the ability to "unilaterally control a governmental taxing entity and use its credit to issue government debt to fund the developer's public infrastructure projects." This is not a bug — it is the design. The developer creates the district, elects themselves to the board (they are the only voters when no residents exist), authorizes the debt, and begins collecting tax revenue before a single homebuyer moves in.
Authorized but unissued metro district debt across Colorado exceeds $1 trillion in public reports. Seven Colorado metro districts hit technical bond defaults in December 2024 alone. The Special District Association of Colorado — which opposed the ethics oversight bill — has ~2,600 special district members, approximately 50% of which are controlled by directors reporting conflicts of interest.
Elections are routinely cancelled because "no candidates filed." Per Change.org petition and resident reports, developers tell residents they can't run until the development is built out — which is false. Any eligible elector can run immediately after purchasing property. The Lorson Ranch election is May 4, 2027. Self-nomination deadline: ~February 26, 2027.
lorsonranchmds.com — HB 25-1219 compliance audit (April 7, 2026)
Direct browser review of lorsonranchmds.com conducted April 7, 2026. HB 25-1219 signed May 29, 2025, effective August 6, 2025. Requirements checked against statute text as summarized by Otten Johnson (ottenjohnson.com).
Homepage requirements partial
✅Board contact info accessible (via Board Meetings page)
✅Meeting dates posted (special meetings as needed)
✅Overlapping entities page exists
✅Call for nominations / how to become board member page exists
⚠️No regular meeting schedule — only virtual special meetings "as needed" — may not meet HB 25-1219 homepage prominence requirement
❌No after-hours emergency contact name/number on homepage
Plain language explanation partial
✅"What is a Metropolitan District?" page exists — legal language but reasonably accessible
✅Public improvements and services page exists
✅Debt capacity page: "$300M aggregate / $80M per district" stated
✅Mill levy cap stated: 50 mills debt service
⚠️No plain-language dollar-amount estimate of taxes for a typical home — only mill levy numbers (requires math)
❌No dollar-amount disclosure of actual current taxes per property as required by HB 25-1219
Board transparency concern
✅Board members listed with names and terms
⚠️Contact email for D1: [email address — contact via district website] — a developer employee, not a district office address
⚠️Contact phone: 719-635-3200 — Landhuis Co. construction office phone, not a district public line
❌No individual contact info for each director — all districts route through same developer contact
❌2 vacant board seats on D1 not addressed or explained
Critical finding — overlapping entities new data
🔍Rolling Hills Ranch Metro Districts Nos. 1–8 listed as overlapping
🔍Colorado Centre Metro District listed as overlapping
🔍Widefield School District #3 — confirms Fountain-Fort Carson schools
🔍Security Fire Protection District — confirms same service area as D6
🔍Widefield Water & Sanitation District — matches D6 service plan filing
Board composition — critical finding from website
District 1, 5, 6, 7 — PresidentJeffrey Mark — [email address — contact via district website] developer
District 1, 5, 6, 7 — Secretary/TreasurerChasity McMorrow — [email address — contact via district website] developer
District 1, 5, 6, 7 — VPGlenn Pawlak — [email address — contact via district website] developer
District 1 — Seats 4 & 5Vacant unfilled
District 2 — PresidentJeffrey Mark — same developer developer
District 2 — 4 other seatsWimberly, Napoleon, Wegener, Galvan — not @landhuisco emails
District 3 — all 5 seatsFredericks, Bailey, Zanders, Mitchell, Allen — appear resident-controlled residents?
District 4 — all 5 seatsPastor, Deegan, Farmer, Stephens, Horwath — appear resident-controlled residents?
Districts 3 and 4 — the two largest bond-issuing districts (~$90M and ~$60M) — appear to have non-Landhuis board members. If these are actual residents, it means residents already control the financing districts with the heaviest debt load. Yet District 1 — the service district that controls operations for all of Lorson Ranch — remains fully developer-controlled. Jeff Mark chairs both District 1 AND District 2, giving Landhuis Co. continued operational control regardless of who sits on Districts 3 and 4.
Bella Mesa Metropolitan District — what it actually is
Source: bellamesametro.specialdistrict.org — direct review April 7, 2026.
LocationCastle Rock, CO — Douglas County (NOT El Paso County)
Organized2004
Size~180 homes — much smaller than Lorson Ranch
Purpose statedInfrastructure, parks, water, sewer, roads
Active projectsRoundabout at Mikelson Blvd + nature trails on 290 vacant acres
Legal managerIcenogle Seaver Pogue, P.C. (ISP Law) — 4725 S. Monaco St., Denver
Board contact — all 5 directors[email address — contact via district website] / (303) 292-9100 — law firm, not individuals
Overlapping entitiesDouglas County Government, Douglas County Re-1 Schools, Capital Metro District
Annual meetingNov 10, 2026 via Zoom — no in-person location
Website platformStreamline — same platform as lorsonranchmds.com
All five Bella Mesa board directors share one law firm email address ([email address — contact via district website]) and one phone number. Residents cannot directly contact any individual director. This is functionally identical to the Lorson Ranch D1 structure where all three filled seats route through Chasity McMorrow's Landhuis Co. email.
How Bella Mesa connects to Lorson Ranch — the industry fact sheet pipeline
The fact sheet you uploaded is Bella Mesa's document. "Colorado Metropolitan Districts' Purpose and Benefits — Fact Sheet" is published at bellamesametro.specialdistrict.org/files/d4932d274/Bella+Mesa+Fact+Sheet-final-ada.pdf. Bella Mesa's website hosts it and their "About Us" page links to it as the explanation of what metro districts are. This is the industry-wide advocacy document — not a Lorson Ranch document specifically — and it claims the "ADA-compliant" designation in its filename while the sites we audited have ADA violations.
Both use the same talking points verbatim. Bella Mesa's About Us page uses identical statistics: "2400+ Colorado metro districts," "87% of new homes sold on the Front Range in 2023 were in a metro district," "90% of Colorado master-planned communities are within a metro district." These came from Zonda Colorado 2023 — an industry market research firm. The same figures appear in the Lorson Ranch service plan presentation by Spencer Fane LLP and in the Bella Mesa fact sheet. It is coordinated industry messaging.
Both use the same website platform. Both lorsonranchmds.com and bellamesametro.specialdistrict.org run on Streamline — "Technology for Special Districts" (getstreamline.com). This is the dominant platform for Colorado metro district websites. Same template, same footer, same structure. The platform itself is used as a compliance tool — districts can check boxes on HB 25-1219 requirements while keeping all director contact info routed through law firm intermediaries.
Same structural problem, different geography. Bella Mesa is in Castle Rock (Douglas County). Lorson Ranch is in El Paso County near Fort Carson. Both have board contact info that routes through a law firm rather than individual elected officials. Both use virtual-only meetings. Both cite the same industry fact sheet to justify their existence. They are templates of the same model deployed county-by-county across Colorado.
Bella Mesa is different in one important way. With only ~180 homes in a built-out community (2004), Bella Mesa's district is in a late-stage operational phase — focused on amenities like trails and a roundabout, not on issuing hundreds of millions in new bonds. It shows what Lorson Ranch will eventually become. Whether Bella Mesa residents actually control their board (versus the law firm controlling access) would require checking the Colorado Secretary of State conflict of interest filings.
Wolfersberger + Bella Mesa + Lorson Ranch — the full picture
No agency oversees metro district elections in Coloradoconfirmed — Wolfersberger
Lorson Ranch May 2027 election — no oversight bodyno enforcement
Lorson Ranch D1 board contact = developer's office phone719-635-3200 = Landhuis
Bella Mesa board contact = law firm phone(303) 292-9100 = ISP Law
Lorson Ranch D3 and D4 appear resident-controlledbut D1 still developer
Ethics oversight bill (HB 25-1079) — failed 3rd consecutive yearMay 2025 — dead
CORA violations — no enforcement agencyconfirmed — Wolfersberger
ADA violations on city housing dashboardsconfirmed — our audit
HB 25-1219 — lorsonranchmds.com partial compliancepartial — gaps identified
The Wolfersberger article, the Lorson Ranch website audit, and the Bella Mesa connection together reveal a coordinated system: metro districts are created using the same legal templates, managed by the same category of law firms, hosted on the same website platform, citing the same industry fact sheets, with

We'll email you a plain-language summary with links to the source documents whenever this office posts new public-record material.
